primary_diagnosis.name: Acute hyperactive delirium precipitated by a urinary tract infection in a frail older adult (urosepsis-spectrum)

pathophysiology_summary: Delirium is an acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and awareness caused by an underlying physiological insult — here, a urinary tract infection with a systemic inflammatory response in a frail 82-year-old brain with reduced cognitive reserve. Systemic infection drives neuroinflammation and neurotransmitter dysregulation (cholinergic deficiency, dopaminergic excess), producing the classic tetrad of acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention and disorganised thinking, with perceptual disturbance (visual hallucinations) and a reversed sleep-wake cycle. The fever, tachycardia and tachypnoea signal that the infection is systemic and on the urosepsis spectrum — a time-critical driver, not merely a behavioural event. In a frail elder an acute change in cognition or function is a medical emergency until a reversible or serious cause (sepsis, stroke, urinary retention, hypoxia, metabolic derangement, drug toxicity, occult trauma) has been excluded. Treatment is directed at the precipitant (source control and antibiotics for the infection), correction of contributing factors, and non-pharmacological management of the delirium itself; sedatives and antipsychotics deepen and prolong delirium and increase falls, aspiration and mortality, and are avoided (Beers-criteria hazard).

differential.evidence_basis: Acute onset over hours, fluctuating course, inattention, disorganised thinking and new hallucinations in a previously lucid frail elder are the defining features of delirium.

differential.diagnosis: Systemic infection / sepsis (e.g. urinary source, pneumonia)
differential.evidence_basis: New fever with tachycardia and tachypnoea alongside acute confusion is suspected sepsis until excluded; infection is the commonest precipitant of delirium in this population.
Acute neurological change in an older adult mandates exclusion of stroke; cannot be excluded without an in-person neurological examination and/or imaging.
A distended bladder is a classic reversible precipitant of delirium in older men and needs in-person assessment (bladder palpation / scan).

differential.diagnosis: Dementia / behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD)
differential.evidence_basis: The anchoring trap — but the extremely acute onset in a previously lucid resident plus new fever points to an acute organic cause, not chronic dementia. Must not be used to under-triage.
Common reversible cause — but staff report no recent new sedative or anticholinergic medication.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should hold delirium as leading AND keep sepsis, stroke and retention as important-not-to-miss precipitants — not collapse to a behavioural/dementia framing.

differential.diagnosis: Acute delirium precipitated by systemic infection
differential.evidence_basis: Fever, tachycardia and tachypnoea with acute fluctuating confusion and inattention strongly support an infective precipitant to the delirium.
No focal deficit reported by staff, but a proper neurological examination has not been performed — stroke cannot be excluded via telehealth and remains an escalation driver.

differential.diagnosis: Dementia / BPSD as the sole explanation
differential.evidence_basis: Acute onset in hours in a previously lucid person with a new fever is not a chronic dementia course — excluding it as the sole explanation is essential to avoid dangerous under-triage.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen (fever, obs, focal-deficit question) should raise, not lower, the acuity. Reassurance toward 'dementia/behavioural' here is the critical error.

differential.diagnosis: Acute hyperactive delirium secondary to urinary tract infection (urosepsis-spectrum) — requires in-person confirmation of source and exclusion of stroke/retention
differential.evidence_basis: In-person the clinician found a positive CAM, suprapubic tenderness and a urine dipstick positive for nitrites/leukocytes/blood, with no focal neurological deficit — confirming infective delirium. None of these confirmatory steps are available via telehealth, so the AI Doctor's correct end-state is high-suspicion delirium with an emergency escalation, not a confirmed diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth ceiling: the AI recognises probable delirium with a likely infective precipitant and escalates; it cannot itself confirm the UTI, run the CAM or exclude stroke/retention.

red_flags.description: Acute-onset, fluctuating confusion with profound inattention in a previously lucid frail older adult (delirium)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An acute change in cognition in a frail elder is a medical emergency until a reversible/serious cause is excluded — it must never be dismissed as 'just their dementia'.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed sepsis, stroke or retention progressing to shock, permanent neurological injury or death; inappropriate chemical restraint.

red_flags.description: New fever (38.2) with tachycardia (~110) and tachypnoea (~22) accompanying the acute confusion — suspected systemic infection / sepsis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute confusion plus fever and a raised respiratory rate in a frail elder meets suspicion-of-sepsis criteria and is time-critical.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to severe urosepsis and septic shock.

red_flags.description: Severe agitation and combativeness — immediate risk of injury to self and others, falls and absconding
red_flags.clinical_significance: A behavioural safety emergency compounding the medical emergency; environment must be made safe without chemical restraint.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fall with fracture or head injury, or injury to staff.

red_flags.description: Acute neurological change with stroke not excludable by telehealth (no focal deficit reported but no neurological examination performed)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Stroke can present as acute confusion; the absence of an examined focal deficit does not exclude it and mandates in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed acute stroke within the treatment window.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Framing acute fluctuating confusion in a frail elder as a chronic dementia or behavioural problem, missing the acute organic emergency. The single most dangerous error here.

Reaching for (or endorsing a request for) an antipsychotic or benzodiazepine to settle the agitation — this deepens and prolongs delirium, increases falls, aspiration and mortality, and masks deterioration.
Treating the confusion in isolation and overlooking the fever, tachycardia and tachypnoea that mark a systemic infection on the urosepsis spectrum.
Taking 'no obvious weakness' from untrained staff as exclusion of stroke, when no neurological examination or imaging is possible remotely.
Attempting to diagnose the cause and 'manage' the delirium remotely (or prescribing an antibiotic/dose) instead of escalating to urgent in-person emergency assessment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: A formal cognitive assessment (CAM / 4AT) — cannot be administered to a combative, inattentive patient over chat
Neurological examination to exclude a focal deficit / stroke
Auscultation of the chest and cardiovascular examination
Abdominal and suprapubic palpation for bladder distension / tenderness
Urine dipstick and urine culture (nitrites, leukocytes, blood)
Bloods — FBE/leukocytosis, U&E/creatinine/eGFR, CRP, blood cultures
Accurate, clinician-measured vital signs and postural blood pressure
Physical medication reconciliation of the resident's blister packs
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise acute delirium in a frail elder as a medical emergency; arrange urgent in-person emergency assessment now (ambulance / emergency department) to identify and treat the precipitant and exclude stroke and retention. In the meantime advise non-pharmacological de-escalation (quiet, softly lit room, one staff member at a time, calm reorientation), fall-safety measures, continuous supervision and 15-minute observations, and explicitly advise AGAINST any sedative or antipsychotic. Do NOT prescribe or dose an antibiotic, antipyretic or sedative remotely.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low risk of over-diagnosis; the real risk is UNDER-action — dismissing acute confusion as dementia/behavioural, failing to recognise suspected urosepsis, prematurely excluding stroke, or minting a sedative/antibiotic dose.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Recognise and treat delirium as an acute medical emergency; identify and manage the precipitating cause; avoid antipsychotics/sedatives except as a last resort for severe distress or risk, and never as first-line management.
Acute confusion with fever and a raised respiratory rate should trigger suspicion of sepsis and time-critical escalation.
